title: Internal Developer Platform (IDP) — System Design
description: Design the platform your own engineers build on. The users are internal developers and the SLOs are developer experience, cognitive load, and deploy velocity. The central move is a developer-facing workload abstraction (a Score-style spec where you declare what a service needs, not how to provision it) backed by a Platform Orchestrator that matches abstract resources to environment-specific definitions and generates config fresh on every deploy. We organize the system as five planes — Developer Control, Integration & Delivery, Resource, Observability, Security — and work the hard parts: config drift vs. dynamic configuration, golden paths vs. flexibility, self-service vs. ticket-ops, multi-tenant isolation, and the one that actually decides success — adoption. A platform nobody uses is a failed platform, so we treat it as a product.

nodes.label: Developer
nodes.description: Internal application engineer who declares what a service needs and ships it.

nodes.label: Platform Engineer
nodes.description: Builds and operates the platform: golden paths, resource definitions, guardrails.

nodes.label: Developer Portal
nodes.description: Self-service UI + software catalog (Backstage/Port): discover, scaffold, view ownership and health.

nodes.label: Workload Spec (Score)
nodes.description: Developer-facing manifest: declare containers + abstract resources (db, dns, route), not cloud-specific config.

nodes.label: Version Control
nodes.description: GitHub/GitLab: source of truth for code and the workload spec; emits push/PR webhooks.

nodes.label: CI Pipeline
nodes.description: Builds, tests, and packages the service into an immutable image on each change.

nodes.label: Artifact Registry
nodes.description: Stores immutable container images / build outputs.

nodes.label: Platform Orchestrator
nodes.description: Reads the spec, matches abstract resources to environment-specific Resource Definitions, generates config per deploy, and drives delivery (Read-Match-Create-Deploy).

nodes.label: Resource Definitions
nodes.description: Operator-authored rules: how each abstract resource type is fulfilled per environment (RDS in prod, a container locally).

nodes.label: Drivers / IaC
nodes.description: Provisioning executors (Terraform/OpenTofu/Crossplane) that create real resources and return outputs (host, port, secret ref).

nodes.label: GitOps CD
nodes.description: Argo CD/Flux: reconciles the live cluster to the generated, version-controlled desired state.

nodes.label: Compute (Kubernetes)
nodes.description: Multi-tenant cluster running each team's workloads in isolated namespaces.

nodes.label: Managed Data
nodes.description: Backing databases/caches provisioned per workload (RDS, Cloud SQL, Redis).

nodes.label: Networking
nodes.description: DNS, ingress/routes, and load balancing bound to each deployed workload.

nodes.label: Messaging
nodes.description: Queues/streams (SQS, Pub/Sub, Kafka) provisioned as workload resources.

nodes.label: Environment Service
nodes.description: Creates on-demand ephemeral/preview environments (one per PR) and reaps stale ones.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.description: Metrics, logs, traces, FinOps, and incident management — wired into every workload by default.

nodes.label: Secrets & Policy
nodes.description: Secret store + injection (Vault/cloud KMS via External Secrets) and policy-as-code guardrails (OPA/Kyverno).

nodes.label: Software Catalog
nodes.description: Inventory of every component: owner, docs, dependencies, and health.

nodes.label: Ticket Queue
nodes.description: Developers request infra and deploys by filing tickets to the ops team.

nodes.label: Ops (manual scripts)
nodes.description: Operators run shared bash/Terraform by hand; knowledge lives in a wiki.

nodes.label: Wiki / Runbooks
nodes.description: Tribal knowledge: how to deploy, which cloud, which services are allowed.

nodes.label: Helm Charts (per service)
nodes.description: Copy-pasted, per-service, per-environment manifests that drift over time.

functional: A developer declares what a service needs — container(s) plus abstract resources (a database, a queue, DNS, a route) — in one spec, with no cloud-specific config.
Self-service from a golden path: scaffold a new service (repo + CI/CD + observability + ownership) and deploy to dev in minutes, with no ticket to the platform team.
The platform provisions and binds backing resources per environment (dev/staging/prod), injecting connection details and secrets automatically.
On-demand ephemeral / preview environments (e.g. one per pull request), automatically torn down.
A software catalog lists every service with its owner, docs, dependencies, and health.
Progressive delivery and rollback are wired in by default via GitOps.
Platform engineers can define and version golden paths and resource definitions, and roll out org standards (e.g. a mandatory APM sidecar) without editing every team's config.
nonFunctional: Low cognitive load: a developer should ship without learning Kubernetes or Terraform (Team Topologies).
Fast time-to-first-deploy (minutes, not days) and fast onboarding of a new developer.
No configuration drift: every environment is reproducible from declared state.
Self-service, not ticket-ops — the platform must not degrade into a request queue that grows with adoption.
Multi-tenant isolation between teams (namespaces + RBAC + quotas; harder isolation for regulated workloads).
Adoption is a first-class requirement: golden paths are paved roads with escape hatches, not mandated cages — measure voluntary adoption.
Reliability of the platform itself: the orchestrator/control plane is a shared failure domain; an outage blocks every team's deploys.
Measurable: DORA delivery metrics, DevEx/SPACE, adoption rate, time-to-first-deploy, and cognitive load.

capacity.label: Internal developers served
capacity.note: Adidas ~300 eng with a 35-person platform team; GitHub internal ~1,300 eng. Independently reported.

capacity.label: Services / components in catalog
capacity.note: Spotify cites 'tens of thousands of components', 700 R&D squads, 120+ internal Backstage plugins.

capacity.label: Deploys per day
capacity.note: Adidas went from a release every 4–6 weeks to 3–4×/day after the platform. K8s case study.

capacity.label: Kubernetes clusters
capacity.note: Mercedes-Benz runs ~900 clusters with ~12 platform engineers (InfoWorld 2024).

capacity.label: Platform team : developers
capacity.note: Practitioner heuristic, not data. First platform team is usually 3–5 engineers.

capacity.label: Time-to-first-deploy target
capacity.note: vs. days of ticket-ops. Vendors cite onboarding a new dev in under an hour (treat as a goal, not a benchmark).

capacity.label: Ephemeral env lifetime
capacity.note: One per open PR; ~76% of non-prod capacity sits idle, so a reaper is mandatory or cost balloons.

capacity.label: Config files (before → after)
capacity.note: 10 services × 4 envs worked-example. Humanitec claims 'up to 95% fewer' — a vendor worked-example, not a benchmark.

capacity.label: DORA platform tradeoff
capacity.note: DORA 2024: IDPs raised individual productivity +6% but could lower delivery throughput −8% and stability −14%. The honest number.

capacity.label: Adoption
capacity.note: Well-designed golden paths report >80% voluntary adoption; mandated platforms breed shadow tooling.

api.description: The primary developer interface. Declare containers, the service port, and abstract resources (a postgres, a dns route) with ${resources.*} placeholders resolved per environment — no cloud detail. The same spec drives local (score-compose) and prod (score-k8s).
api.response: (committed to the repo; consumed by the orchestrator on each push)

api.description: Submit a spec + image for an environment. The orchestrator matches each abstract resource to a Resource Definition, invokes drivers to provision, generates config, and triggers GitOps delivery.
Create a new service from a golden path (Backstage Scaffolder-style): scaffolds the repo, CI, catalog entry, ownership, and a starter spec.
Query the software catalog: components, owners, dependencies, links, and health.
Platform-engineer-facing: register how an abstract resource type is fulfilled per environment (matching rules + driver).
Create an on-demand ephemeral/preview environment (e.g. for a PR); auto-deleted on PR close or by the reaper TTL.

dataModel.name: workloads
dataModel.note: An application/service registered on the platform.

dataModel.name: workload_specs
dataModel.note: Versioned Score-style spec for a workload.

dataModel.name: resource_definitions
dataModel.note: How an abstract resource type is fulfilled per environment.

dataModel.name: deployments
dataModel.note: A workload × environment × spec version release.

dataModel.name: golden_paths
dataModel.note: A versioned scaffolder template and the standards it bakes in.

patterns.name: Workload specification
patterns.what: Developers declare what a service needs (containers + abstract resources), not how to provision it.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever you want to hide cloud/Kubernetes detail behind a stable developer contract.

patterns.name: Separation of concerns (what vs. how)
patterns.what: Developers own the 'what' (the spec); platform engineers own the 'how' (resource definitions). The orchestrator computes the rest.
patterns.whenToUse: To let both sides evolve independently without coordinating on every change.

patterns.name: Dynamic Configuration Management
patterns.what: Generate app + infra config fresh per deploy per environment instead of maintaining static templates.
patterns.whenToUse: When per-environment, copy-pasted config has started to drift.

patterns.name: Golden paths (paved roads)
patterns.what: The supported, best-practices-baked way to build and ship a service, self-served in seconds.
patterns.whenToUse: To make the right way the easy way without mandating it.

patterns.name: Platform-as-a-Product
patterns.what: Treat the IDP as a product with internal developers as customers: discovery, roadmap, UX, adoption metrics.
patterns.whenToUse: Always — a platform nobody adopts is a failed platform.

patterns.name: Thinnest Viable Platform
patterns.what: Build the minimum platform that measurably accelerates teams; never thicker than necessary.
patterns.whenToUse: At the start, and as a constant check against over-building.

patterns.name: Software catalog
patterns.what: A single inventory of all components with ownership, docs, dependencies, and health.
patterns.whenToUse: Once you have more services than anyone can hold in their head.

patterns.name: GitOps
patterns.what: Git is the source of truth for desired state; an agent continuously reconciles the cluster to it.

patterns.name: Control-plane reconciliation vs. apply-time IaC
patterns.what: Continuously reconciling controllers (Crossplane) auto-correct drift; apply-time CLIs (Terraform) reconcile to a point in time.
patterns.whenToUse: When choosing the engine under the orchestrator.

patterns.name: Environment-as-a-Service
patterns.what: On-demand, short-lived environments provisioned and torn down automatically.
patterns.whenToUse: For per-PR previews and isolated testing without manual env management.

patterns.name: Policy-as-code (guardrails)
patterns.what: Encode org policy as code that redirects toward safe defaults rather than blocking.
patterns.whenToUse: To enforce standards without becoming a gate.

patterns.name: Soft multi-tenancy (namespace + RBAC + quotas)
patterns.what: Isolate teams with namespaces, RBAC, NetworkPolicies, and quotas on a shared cluster.
patterns.whenToUse: For mutually trusting internal teams; escalate to hard isolation for regulated workloads.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Tickets, Shared Scripts, and a Wiki Runbook
description: Start where most orgs actually are. A developer who needs a database, a deploy, or a new service files a ticket. The ops team runs shared bash and Terraform by hand, following a wiki runbook that encodes the tribal knowledge: which cloud, which approved services, which steps in which order.

This works for a handful of services. But every new service and every environment multiplies the ops team's manual toil, and the request queue grows linearly with adoption — the classic ticket-ops bottleneck (CNCF calls this the 'Provisional' maturity level: reactive, by-request, implementations out of date).

view.caption: Developers request infra by ticket; ops provisions by hand from a runbook. It does not scale with the number of teams.

whyNow: This is the baseline whose pain motivates everything that follows. Name the bottleneck explicitly before solving it.

recap.after: Infra and deploys happen — slowly, by ticket.
recap.newRisk: Lead times in days; the request queue grows with every new team; knowledge is tribal and goes stale.

traps.trap: Rename 'Ops' to 'Platform Team' and call it platform engineering.
traps.why: The reactive request queue is the actual problem; relabeling it doesn't remove it — the queue still grows with adoption.
traps.instead: Attack the bottleneck with self-service: developers provision without a human in the loop.

traps.trap: Document your way out with a bigger, better wiki.
traps.why: Runbooks rot the moment infra changes, and a human still has to execute them by hand.
traps.instead: Encode the runbook as automation (golden paths) so the right way is the executed way.

steps.title: 2. Automate Delivery: CI/CD Pipelines + Helm Charts per Service
description: Replace manual deploys with pipelines: on each change, CI builds, tests, and packages an immutable image into a registry, and a GitOps CD agent reconciles the cluster to a desired state described in Git. New service? Copy the Helm chart, tweak the values, repeat.

Deploys are now automated and auditable — a real step up. But the copy-paste model plants the seed of the next crisis: 10 services × 4 environments is 300+ YAML files, and every copy drifts from the others over time. Templating harder doesn't fix drift; it spreads it.

view.caption: CI builds an immutable image; GitOps CD reconciles the cluster from per-service Helm charts.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which CI and which GitOps CD — and why is more Helm not the answer to drift?

options.name: GitHub Actions + Argo CD (default)
options.description: Managed CI close to the repo, plus Argo CD for GitOps delivery with a web UI and ApplicationSet for multi-tenant/preview generation.
pros: No CI control-plane to operate; runs next to the code.
Argo CD has a UI, SSO, and ApplicationSet (templated multi-cluster + PR-preview generation).
Native multi-tenancy via its own API server.
cons: Argo CD is monolithic; you operate it.
Actions minutes and self-hosted runners still need cost/security attention.

view.caption: Managed CI plus Argo CD GitOps — the common, batteries-included starting point.

options.name: GitLab CI + Flux
options.description: GitLab's integrated CI with Flux for GitOps reconciliation.
pros: One vendor for repo + CI; fast to stand up for existing GitLab teams.
Flux is composable controllers you adopt incrementally; delegates access control to Kubernetes RBAC.
cons: Flux has no UI and no ApplicationSet equivalent — more cluster-admin burden.
Tighter coupling to the GitLab ecosystem.

view.caption: Integrated GitLab CI plus Flux — composable and RBAC-driven, no UI.

options.name: Jenkins (self-hosted)
options.description: A self-hosted Jenkins controller with a plugin-driven pipeline.
pros: Fully self-hosted; 1,500+ plugins; still the backbone of much of the Fortune 500.
cons: Plugins are a large attack/maintenance surface.
Controller is a scaling and availability bottleneck; persistent agents drift from hermetic.

view.caption: Self-hosted Jenkins — maximum control, maximum operational surface.

concepts.definition: Git holds the desired system state; an agent continuously reconciles the live cluster to match it.
concepts.whyItMatters: Delivery becomes declarative, auditable, and reversible — roll back by reverting a commit.
concepts.example: Argo CD watches a repo and applies the manifests it finds there.

concepts.definition: When running config diverges from declared config because copies were edited independently.
concepts.whyItMatters: Drift is the root cause of 'works in staging, breaks in prod' and is what dynamic configuration later eliminates.
concepts.example: Two services' Helm values that started identical and slowly diverged across four environments.

whyNow: Automating delivery is the prerequisite for everything above it — but it surfaces the YAML-sprawl problem that forces the orchestrator.

recap.after: Every commit builds an immutable image and GitOps reconciles it to the cluster.
recap.newRisk: Copy-paste Helm: 300+ config files that drift; the platform team still hand-holds every new service.

traps.trap: Template your way out of drift with ever-fancier Helm.
traps.why: More templating multiplies the files that can drift; it manages the symptom, not the cause.
traps.instead: Treat configuration as data generated per deploy, not as static files to maintain.

steps.title: 3. A Self-Service Portal over Existing Tooling (Backstage)
description: Give developers one front door: a software catalog of every service (owner, docs, dependencies, health), scaffolder templates that codify golden paths, and links to the pipelines they already have. Developers can now discover and scaffold in seconds instead of asking around.

But a portal is a discovery and interaction layer — it cannot, by itself, provision infrastructure. 'Backstage is not your platform': developers can find and scaffold, yet the real provisioning still happens through the pipeline-and-Helm machinery underneath. This is the step where you decide build-vs-buy for the portal, and recognize that the portal needs an engine beneath it.

view.caption: A portal adds a catalog and golden-path scaffolding on top of existing CI/CD — discovery, not provisioning.

steps.decisionPrompt: Build Backstage, buy a SaaS portal, or use a supported distribution — and what does the portal NOT solve?

options.name: Self-host Backstage (default for large orgs)
options.description: Backstage is a framework, not a product: you build and operate your portal on it.
pros: Maximum flexibility; the de-facto open standard (Spotify → CNCF).
Owns the catalog, scaffolder, and TechDocs.
cons: A framework, not turnkey: ~6–12 months to production, ~2–5 FTEs ongoing.
Upstream ships no enterprise support, lifecycle, or back-patches.

view.caption: Self-hosted Backstage — flexible, but a platform team has to run it.

options.name: SaaS portal (Port / Cortex / OpsLevel)
options.description: An opinionated, hosted internal developer portal with a configurable data model.
pros: A catalog in weeks, not a platform team running a portal.
Less to operate; built-in scorecards and integrations.
cons: Less flexible than raw Backstage; you adopt their data model.
SaaS data-residency and per-seat cost considerations.

view.caption: A SaaS portal trades flexibility for speed-to-value.

options.name: Red Hat Developer Hub (supported Backstage)
options.description: Red Hat's supported enterprise build of Backstage: RBAC, audit logging, verified plugins, timely patches.
pros: Backstage without owning upstream lifecycle and security patching.
Enterprise support and adoption analytics.
cons: Vendor-curated plugin set; subscription cost.
Still your responsibility to author golden paths and keep the catalog clean.

view.caption: A supported Backstage distribution — the middle path between build and buy.

concepts.definition: A central inventory of all components — services, libraries, APIs — with ownership and metadata.
concepts.whyItMatters: It answers 'who owns this and what depends on it?' once you have more services than anyone can hold in their head.
concepts.example: Backstage's catalog, populated from catalog-info.yaml in each repo.

concepts.definition: The supported, best-practices-baked way to build and ship a service, offered as a template.
concepts.whyItMatters: It makes the right way the easy way — developers self-serve a production-ready scaffold in seconds.
concepts.example: A 'Go gRPC service' template that scaffolds repo + CI + observability + ownership.

concepts.definition: Treating the platform as a product with internal developers as its customers.
concepts.whyItMatters: Adoption — not feature count — decides success; you need discovery, UX, and metrics, not just a tool.
concepts.example: Running user research and a roadmap for the portal instead of shipping it and hoping.

whyNow: The portal is the developer-facing surface, but installing it before the provisioning engine produces a pretty catalog over the same old ticket-ops.

recap.before: Automated delivery, but discovery and scaffolding are manual and tribal.
recap.after: One self-service front door: catalog, golden-path scaffolding, ownership, and docs.
recap.newRisk: A portal is not a platform — it can't provision infra; the engine underneath is still copy-paste Helm.

traps.trap: Build the front-end first and call the portal 'the platform'.
traps.why: A catalog can't provision infrastructure; you've shipped a directory, not self-service.
traps.instead: Put automation and an orchestrator underneath — a clunky CLI that automates a 3-hour task beats a beautiful portal that does nothing.

traps.trap: Stand up Backstage expecting a turnkey product.
traps.why: Backstage is a framework with no enterprise support upstream; under-resourcing it strands a half-built portal.
traps.instead: Budget 2–5 FTEs, or buy a SaaS/supported distribution instead.

steps.title: 4. The Workload Spec + Platform Orchestrator (the key abstraction)
description: This is the heart of the design. Introduce a Score-style workload spec: the developer declares what a service needs — containers plus abstract resources (a postgres, a dns route, a queue) — with ${resources.*} placeholders and no cloud-specific detail. Platform engineers separately author Resource Definitions: how each abstract type is fulfilled per environment (RDS in prod, a container locally).

On every push, the Platform Orchestrator runs Read-Match-Create-Deploy: it reads the spec, matches each abstract resource to the right Resource Definition for that environment/project/resource-class, invokes Drivers (Terraform/OpenTofu/Crossplane) to provision the real thing, wires the outputs (host, port, secret ref) back into the workload, and hands the generated desired state to GitOps. Config is generated fresh per deploy — Dynamic Configuration Management — so there are no static per-env files to drift.

This is the move that turns 'Backstage + a pile of Helm charts' into a real platform: developers express needs once, platform engineers express realization once per environment, and the orchestrator computes everything in between.

view.caption: The developer declares needs in a spec; the orchestrator matches them to per-environment definitions, provisions via drivers, and generates config for delivery.

steps.decisionPrompt: Buy an orchestrator (Humanitec), self-operate one (Kratix), build on a control plane (Crossplane), or assemble Score + your own controllers?

options.name: Humanitec Platform Orchestrator (buy)
options.description: A SaaS, SLA-backed orchestrator with a shared resource graph and API/CLI/Portal interfaces.
pros: No control plane to operate; SLA-backed.
Graph-based resource matching and blast-radius analysis out of the box.
cons: Vendor dependency and cost; a hosted control plane in your delivery path.
Some 'magic' to learn and trust.

view.caption: A managed orchestrator — least to operate, most vendor coupling.

options.name: Kratix (self-operated, K8s-native)
options.description: An open-source framework you install and operate; its interface is the Kubernetes API. Complementary to Crossplane.
pros: Open-source, no vendor lock-in; 'Promises' model maps cleanly to platform APIs.
Runs in your cluster; composes with Crossplane underneath.
cons: You install, operate, harden, and back it up.
K8s-API-only interface — less turnkey for non-K8s developers.

view.caption: A self-operated, open-source orchestrator on Kubernetes.

options.name: Score + your own controllers (assemble)
options.description: Adopt the vendor-neutral Score spec and build the matching/generation engine yourself with GitOps.
pros: Maximum control; Score is CNCF-Sandbox and genuinely decoupled (score-humanitec is deprecated).
No new platform vendor; reuse Argo/Flux you already run.
cons: You build and own the matching engine — significant, ongoing engineering.
Easy to under-build the resource-definition and drift story.

view.caption: Open spec plus a home-built engine — most control, most work.

concepts.definition: A vendor-neutral spec (score.yaml) where a developer declares containers + abstract resource dependencies, decoupled from any platform.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the stable developer contract that hides Kubernetes and cloud detail; the same spec drives local and prod.
concepts.example: resources: { db: { type: postgres } } — no mention of RDS or a connection string.

concepts.definition: A configuration engine that reads the spec, matches abstract resources to environment-specific Resource Definitions, and generates concrete config per deploy.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the heart of the IDP — where developer intent becomes real infrastructure.

concepts.definition: An operator-authored rule that says how an abstract resource type is fulfilled in a given environment.
concepts.whyItMatters: It encodes the 'how' once per environment so developers never have to.
concepts.example: type: postgres + match {env: prod} → driver terraform/rds.

concepts.definition: Generating app + infra config fresh per deploy per environment rather than maintaining static files.
concepts.whyItMatters: It eliminates config drift at the root: there are no long-lived per-env files to diverge.
concepts.example: The same spec produces docker-compose locally (score-compose) and K8s manifests in prod (score-k8s).

whyNow: Without an orchestrator the portal is a facade over copy-paste Helm. This abstraction is what makes self-service real and kills config drift.

recap.before: A portal over copy-paste Helm — discovery without true self-service.
recap.after: Developers declare needs once; the orchestrator provisions and generates config per environment automatically.
recap.newRisk: The orchestrator becomes a shared failure domain and a powerful abstraction that can hide too much if over-applied.

traps.trap: Over-abstract — hide everything behind the spec ('golden cage').
traps.why: An abstraction designed for control, not consumption, boxes power users in and breeds shadow tooling.
traps.instead: Paved road WITH escape hatches: solve the 80% case, let advanced teams reach raw infra.

traps.trap: Ship a leaky abstraction that swallows the real error.
traps.why: Developers get '5-minute deploys, 4-hour debugs' when the wrapper hides the underlying Kubernetes/cloud error.
traps.instead: Surface underlying errors transparently; the abstraction should add clarity, not obscure it.

traps.trap: Treat the orchestrator as infallible and put it directly in the critical path with no isolation.
traps.why: It is a shared control plane — if it's down, every team's deploys stop.
traps.instead: Isolate failure, mirror read paths, and decouple build from deploy so an orchestrator outage degrades gracefully.

flows.name: git push → provision → deploy (Read-Match-Create-Deploy)
flows.note: How one commit becomes running infrastructure with config generated on the fly.

deepDives.title: Why config-as-data beats templates
points: Static per-env templates rot: 10 services × 4 envs ≈ 300+ files, each editable independently, so they drift.
The orchestrator routes a resource request to the right IaC module by context (env, project, resource class) — no human in the loop.
A live resource graph surfaces drift immediately and supports blast-radius analysis before a change.
Honest caveat: Humanitec's '~90–95% fewer config files' is a vendor worked-example; the mechanism is sound, the exact number is marketing.

strong: Names the spec as the developer contract and the orchestrator as the engine, and keeps the 'what vs. how' split crisp.
Explains config-as-data as the cure for drift, not just 'we use Terraform'.
Volunteers the orchestrator-as-SPOF risk and the over-abstraction trap unprompted.
weak: Treats the orchestrator as a magic box without explaining match→provision→inject.
Conflates the portal with the orchestrator, or thinks more Helm/templating solves drift.

steps.title: 4a. Choose the IaC Engine Under the Orchestrator
description: The orchestrator's drivers need an engine to actually create cloud resources. The cleanest tradeoff to reason about is control-plane reconciliation vs. apply-time CLI: Crossplane runs as Kubernetes controllers that continuously reconcile and auto-correct drift (state in etcd, no state file); Terraform/Pulumi are apply-time tools that reconcile to a point in time when you run them.

License history is now part of the decision: Terraform moved to the BSL in Aug 2023 (IBM closed its HashiCorp acquisition in Feb 2025), which spawned OpenTofu, a Linux Foundation MPL fork that has since diverged (native state encryption, provider-defined functions).

view.caption: The driver layer provisions concrete resources; its engine choice shapes drift handling and licensing.

options.name: Terraform / OpenTofu (apply-time, default)
options.description: Mature, declarative IaC with a huge provider ecosystem; OpenTofu is the open MPL fork after Terraform's BSL move.
pros: Ubiquitous skills and providers; the orchestrator just calls modules.
OpenTofu keeps it open-source with state encryption.
cons: Apply-time: reconciles only when run, so out-of-band drift lingers until the next apply.
State-file management and locking to operate.

options.name: Crossplane (control-plane reconciliation)
options.description: Kubernetes controllers that continuously reconcile cloud resources and auto-correct drift; no state file.
pros: Continuous drift correction; resources are K8s objects with RBAC.
Graduated CNCF (Nov 2025); composes with Kratix.
cons: You run and harden the control plane and providers.
Smaller provider coverage than Terraform; a steeper model.

options.name: Pulumi (apply-time, real languages)
options.description: IaC in general-purpose languages with a Kubernetes operator for reconciliation.
pros: Real languages and testing; good for complex logic.
Operator enables continuous reconciliation if desired.
cons: Smaller community than Terraform; language flexibility can reduce consistency.
Still fundamentally apply-time without the operator.

concepts.definition: Crossplane continuously reconciles via controllers; Terraform/Pulumi reconcile to a point in time when you run apply.
concepts.whyItMatters: It decides whether out-of-band drift self-heals or waits for the next apply — the single cleanest IaC tradeoff to teach.
concepts.example: Someone edits a security group by hand: Crossplane reverts it; Terraform shows a diff only on the next plan.

whyNow: The orchestrator is only as good as the engine it drives; this is where drift-handling and the open-source/licensing reality get decided.

recap.before: Abstract resources matched but not yet provisioned by a concrete engine.
recap.after: Drivers provision real cloud resources via a chosen IaC engine.
recap.newRisk: Apply-time engines leave drift until the next run; control-plane engines add a control plane to operate.

steps.title: 5. The Resource Plane: Compute, Data, Networking — and Multi-Tenancy
description: Now define what the orchestrator provisions into: Kubernetes compute, managed databases and caches, DNS/ingress, and messaging. The defining design question here is multi-tenant isolation — how teams share the cluster without stepping on each other.

For mutually trusting internal teams (the common IDP case), namespace-per-team plus RBAC, NetworkPolicies, and resource quotas is adequate — this is the neutral Kubernetes guidance, not just vendor marketing. Reserve hard isolation (a virtual or dedicated control plane per tenant, e.g. vcluster) for untrusted tenants or regulated workloads (HIPAA/PCI), because cluster-per-tenant carries high provisioning overhead and poor cost efficiency.

view.caption: The resource plane: workloads run on a multi-tenant cluster with managed data, networking, and messaging bound per workload.

steps.decisionPrompt: Soft isolation (namespaces + RBAC) or hard isolation (virtual/dedicated clusters) — for whom?

options.name: Namespace-per-team + RBAC + quotas (default)
options.description: Soft multi-tenancy on a shared cluster: a namespace per team, RBAC, NetworkPolicies, and resource quotas.
pros: Cost-efficient; one control plane to operate.
Adequate isolation for mutually trusting internal teams (per Kubernetes docs).
cons: Shared kernel and control plane; noisy-neighbor risk; cluster-scoped CRDs can't be self-served per tenant.
A misconfigured tenant can affect the cluster.

options.name: vcluster (virtual control plane per tenant)
options.description: Each tenant gets a virtual cluster with its own API server/etcd on shared nodes.
pros: Tenants get cluster-scoped resources and CRDs with a tiny footprint.
Stronger isolation without a full cluster per team.
cons: More moving parts to operate than plain namespaces.
Still shares the host kernel; not full hardware isolation.

options.name: Cluster-per-tenant (hard isolation)
options.description: A dedicated cluster per tenant for regulated or untrusted workloads.
pros: Strongest isolation and blast-radius containment.
Independent upgrade and compliance boundaries.
cons: Very high provisioning overhead; poor cost efficiency.
Many clusters to patch and observe.

concepts.definition: Soft = shared control plane isolated by namespaces/RBAC/quotas; hard = separate control plane or cluster per tenant.
concepts.whyItMatters: It is the central cost-vs-isolation tradeoff of the resource plane.
concepts.example: Trusting internal teams → namespaces; a PCI workload → its own cluster.

concepts.whyItMatters: It removes 'works on my machine' and manual environment management.
concepts.example: A full preview environment created per pull request.

whyNow: The orchestrator needs somewhere to provision into; isolation decisions here shape security, cost, and the blast radius of mistakes.

recap.before: Resources matched and provisioned in the abstract.
recap.after: Workloads run on a multi-tenant cluster with data, networking, and messaging bound per workload.
recap.newRisk: Soft isolation trusts tenants not to misbehave; the wrong isolation choice is either insecure or wildly expensive.

steps.title: 6. Ephemeral Environments + Golden-Path Versioning
description: Turn the resource plane into Environment-as-a-Service. When a PR opens, an environment service asks the orchestrator to stand up a full preview environment (Argo CD's ApplicationSet PR generator creates one Application per open PR; vcluster gives each PR a virtual cluster). On close, it's deleted.

Two failure modes to design against. First, cost: PR-close cleanup alone leaks money, because branches get abandoned — you need an external reaper (a CronJob) enforcing a TTL, since ~76% of non-prod capacity sits idle. Second, change management: version golden paths with semver, make breaking (v2) changes opt-in, and run versions in parallel during deprecation. Never force a big-bang migration — that's how you lose adoption.

view.caption: A PR triggers an on-demand environment via the orchestrator; a reaper tears down stale ones.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you give every PR an environment without the cloud bill — and evolve golden paths without forcing migrations?

options.name: Argo CD ApplicationSet PR generator (default)
options.description: An Application per open PR, auto-created and auto-deleted, reusing your existing Argo CD.
pros: Native to Argo CD; no new system.
Auto-cleanup on PR close.
cons: Shares the cluster — needs quotas and a reaper for abandoned branches.
Cluster-scoped resources still collide across PRs.

options.name: vcluster per PR
options.description: Each PR gets a full virtual cluster on shared nodes.
pros: Full cluster semantics (CRDs, cluster-scoped objects) per PR.
Strong isolation with a small footprint.
cons: More to operate; still needs a TTL reaper.
Per-PR provisioning latency.

concepts.definition: A scheduled job that deletes environments past a TTL regardless of PR state.
concepts.whyItMatters: PR-close cleanup misses abandoned branches; without a reaper, non-prod spend balloons.
concepts.example: A nightly CronJob deleting any preview env older than 48h.

concepts.definition: Semver the path: v1.x is non-breaking; v2.0 breaking changes are opt-in, with versions running in parallel.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets the platform evolve without forcing a migration that kills voluntary adoption.
concepts.example: Teams stay on go-service@v1 until they choose to adopt @v2.

whyNow: Ephemeral envs are the payoff of self-service; without cost control and versioning, they either bankrupt you or freeze the platform.

recap.before: Permanent environments only; golden paths change in place.
recap.after: Every PR gets a disposable environment; golden paths evolve via opt-in versions.
recap.newRisk: Idle preview envs leak cost without a reaper; forced migrations erode adoption.

traps.why: Abandoned branches never close, so their environments live forever and the cloud bill balloons.
traps.instead: Add an external TTL reaper that deletes stale envs regardless of PR state.

traps.trap: Force every team onto the new golden path on a deadline ('railroad').
traps.why: Mandated migration breeds resentment and shadow tooling; a railroad forces one path and removes autonomy.
traps.instead: Guardrail, not railroad: version paths, make breaking changes opt-in, run versions in parallel.

steps.title: 7. Bake In Observability & Security — and Measure the Platform
description: Make the Observability and Security planes part of the golden path so every service gets them by default, not as an afterthought. Observability: metrics, logs, traces, FinOps, incident management. Security: secrets via a store + injection (Vault/OpenBao or a cloud KMS, synced with the External Secrets Operator — which often makes a self-hosted Vault optional), policy-as-code guardrails (OPA/Gatekeeper or Kyverno), identity, and code analysis.

Then measure the platform like a product. Combine DORA delivery metrics, the SPACE and DevEx frameworks (feedback loops, cognitive load, flow), adoption rate, and time-to-first-deploy. Be honest: DORA 2024 found IDPs raised individual productivity (+6%) but could lower delivery throughput (−8%) and stability (−14%). Platforms are a tradeoff you manage with data, not a guaranteed win — and you optimize for developer time, never vanity metrics like portal logins.

view.caption: Observability and security are wired into every workload by default; health flows back to the catalog and informs the platform's own metrics.

steps.decisionPrompt: Self-host the secrets/policy/observability stack or use managed services — and what do you actually measure?

options.name: Cloud-managed + External Secrets Operator (default)
options.description: Cloud KMS/Secrets Manager + ESO syncing into K8s, managed observability, Kyverno for policy.
pros: ESO + cloud KMS often makes a self-hosted Vault unnecessary.
Less to operate; pay-as-you-go observability.
cons: Per-cloud coupling; cost at scale.
Managed observability bills can surprise you (hence FinOps).

options.name: Self-hosted (Vault/OpenBao + Prometheus/Grafana + OPA)
options.description: Run your own secrets, metrics, and policy stack.
pros: Full control and portability; no per-service SaaS fees.
OPA/Gatekeeper enforces policy across K8s, CI, and Terraform.
cons: You operate Vault HA, Prometheus retention, and Rego.
Vault moved to BSL (Aug 2023) — OpenBao is the open fork.

concepts.definition: Encode org policy as code that redirects toward safe defaults instead of blocking outright.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guardrails enforce standards without turning the platform into a gate that developers route around.
concepts.example: Kyverno can mutate a missing label onto a resource; Gatekeeper validates at admission.

concepts.definition: Bind secrets into a workload at deploy time from a store rather than committing them.
concepts.whyItMatters: It keeps credentials out of specs and Git while still wiring them into the running service.
concepts.example: ESO syncs a cloud secret into a K8s Secret the orchestrator references.

concepts.definition: Complementary measures: DORA (delivery), SPACE (productivity dimensions), DevEx (feedback loops, cognitive load, flow).
concepts.whyItMatters: No single number captures a platform; combining them — plus adoption — avoids vanity metrics.
concepts.example: Track deploy frequency and change-fail rate alongside developer-survey cognitive load.

whyNow: Observability and security belong in the golden path so they're universal; and without measurement you can't tell a platform that helps from one that quietly hurts throughput.

recap.before: Self-service delivery onto isolated, ephemeral environments.
recap.after: Every workload ships with observability and security by default, and the platform is measured like a product.
recap.newRisk: Measured honestly, platforms can trade some throughput/stability for productivity — manage it, don't deny it.

traps.trap: Measure success by portal logins and API call counts.
traps.why: Vanity metrics go up while developer outcomes don't; they hide whether the platform actually helps.
traps.instead: Track DORA + DevEx + adoption + time-to-first-deploy — outcomes, not activity.

traps.trap: Mandate adoption to hit a target ('platform as a gate').
traps.why: Forced use suppresses the feedback that tells you the platform is wrong, and breeds shadow tooling.
traps.instead: Drive voluntary pull; treat low adoption as a product bug to fix, not a compliance failure.

traps.trap: Run an ivory-tower platform team that doesn't dogfood.
traps.why: With admin bypass the team says 'works fine for us' while developers hit 3-week onboarding and cryptic failures.
traps.instead: Dogfood under the same constraints as your developers.

strong: Bakes observability/security into the golden path rather than bolting on later.
Cites the DORA 2024 platform tradeoff and frames the platform as a product to be measured.
Knows ESO + cloud KMS can replace a self-hosted Vault.
weak: Treats security/observability as a later add-on.
Claims platforms always make teams faster, or measures only vanity metrics.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: The full five-plane IDP. In the Developer Control Plane a developer uses the portal and a Score-style spec to declare what a service needs. The Integration & Delivery Plane turns that into reality: CI builds an image, the Platform Orchestrator matches abstract resources to per-environment Resource Definitions, drivers provision via IaC, and GitOps CD reconciles the cluster. The Resource Plane runs the workloads on multi-tenant compute with managed data, networking, and messaging. The Observability and Security planes are baked into the golden path so every workload gets metrics, traces, secrets, and policy by default — and telemetry flows back to the catalog and the platform's own product metrics.

view.caption: The five-plane Internal Developer Platform: intent flows down from the spec; telemetry flows back up.

functional.requirement: Declare service needs in one spec, no cloud detail
functional.how: A Score-style workload spec with abstract resources resolved per environment.

functional.requirement: Self-service: scaffold + deploy in minutes, no ticket
functional.how: Portal golden paths scaffold the service; the orchestrator provisions on push.

functional.requirement: Auto-provision and bind resources per environment
functional.how: Resource Definitions + drivers provision and inject outputs/secrets per environment.
An environment service creates a full env per PR and a reaper tears down stale ones.
The portal's catalog tracks owner, docs, dependencies, and health.
GitOps CD reconciles desired state and rolls back by reverting a commit.

functional.requirement: Platform engineers version golden paths and standards
functional.how: Versioned scaffolder templates and Resource Definitions roll out org standards centrally.

nonFunctional.how: The spec hides Kubernetes/Terraform; developers express needs, not infrastructure.
Golden-path scaffolding + automatic provisioning take minutes, not days.
Config is generated fresh per deploy (Dynamic Configuration Management), not maintained as static files.
The orchestrator removes the human from the provisioning loop.
Namespaces + RBAC + quotas by default; virtual/dedicated clusters for regulated tenants.
Paved roads with escape hatches, opt-in versioning, and product management.

nonFunctional.requirement: Platform reliability (control plane not a SPOF)
nonFunctional.how: Failure isolation and decoupling build from deploy keep an orchestrator outage from blocking all teams.
DORA + SPACE + DevEx + adoption + time-to-first-deploy, tracked like a product.

tradeoff: Self-host Backstage when you have 2–5 FTEs and need deep customization; buy a SaaS portal when you want a catalog in weeks.
Red Hat Developer Hub is the supported middle path.
makesIrrelevant: A SaaS portal removes the need to operate Backstage upstream lifecycle and patching.

tradeoff: Buy Humanitec to avoid running a control plane; self-operate Kratix for open-source control; assemble Score + controllers for maximum ownership.
Score is vendor-neutral (CNCF Sandbox); score-humanitec is deprecated.
makesIrrelevant: A managed orchestrator with dynamic config generation removes hand-maintained per-environment config files.

tradeoff: Use apply-time Terraform/OpenTofu for ubiquity; Crossplane when you want continuous drift correction as K8s controllers.
OpenTofu is the open MPL fork after Terraform's BSL move (Aug 2023).
makesIrrelevant: Crossplane's continuous reconciliation removes the need for periodic drift-detection runs.

tradeoff: Use managed CI (GitHub Actions / GitLab CI) close to the repo; self-host Jenkins only when you need its plugin ecosystem or full control.
Argo CD has a UI, SSO, and ApplicationSet (multi-tenant + PR previews); Flux is composable controllers with RBAC-only access and no UI.
makesIrrelevant: GitOps reconciliation removes imperative deploy scripts.

tradeoff: Cloud-managed registries win on IAM integration and pull speed when you're already on that cloud; Harbor (CNCF) for self-hosted scanning/replication.
Cloud KMS + External Secrets Operator covers most K8s teams; run Vault/OpenBao when you need dynamic secrets or cloud-agnostic control.
Vault moved to BSL (Aug 2023); OpenBao is the open fork.
makesIrrelevant: External Secrets Operator + a cloud KMS makes a self-hosted Vault optional for many teams.

tradeoff: OPA/Gatekeeper (Rego) enforces policy beyond K8s (CI, Terraform, Envoy); Kyverno (YAML) can mutate and generate K8s resources and is easier for K8s-only policy.
Use a managed Kubernetes service unless you have a strong on-prem/regulatory reason to self-manage the control plane.
makesIrrelevant: Managed Kubernetes removes control-plane operations and upgrades.

tradeoff: Namespaces + RBAC for trusting internal teams; vcluster for stronger isolation with a small footprint; a cluster per tenant for regulated/untrusted workloads.
Self-host Prometheus/Grafana/OTel for control and cost; managed observability (Datadog/New Relic) for less ops at a higher bill — add FinOps to watch it.

say: Clarify the users are internal developers and the goal is self-service, low-cognitive-load delivery — not a customer-facing product.
State the hardest constraints up front: no config drift, no ticket-ops bottleneck, and adoption (a platform nobody uses is a failed platform).
Name the five planes as the structure; size organizationally (developers, services, deploys/day, clusters), not in packets/sec.
Set the real SLOs: time-to-first-deploy in minutes, reproducible environments, measurable DevEx.
Walk the maturity arc — tickets → CI/CD+Helm → portal → orchestrator — and name the forcing function at each stage.
Land on the key move: a workload spec (what) + orchestrator matching to Resource Definitions (how) generating config per deploy.
Explain Read-Match-Create-Deploy and config-as-data as the cure for drift.
Cover build/buy (Humanitec vs. Kratix vs. Score+controllers) and the IaC engine choice (control-plane vs. apply-time).
Volunteer the risks: orchestrator-as-SPOF, over-abstraction/golden cage, ephemeral-env cost, and the DORA tradeoff (+6% productivity, −8% throughput, −14% stability).
Close on platform-as-a-product: paved roads with escape hatches, opt-in versioning, and measuring adoption.

expectations: Names the five planes and can place common tools (Backstage, Argo CD, Terraform, Vault) in them.
Understands self-service beats filing tickets, and that a portal gives developers one front door.
Designs the workload-spec → orchestrator → Resource-Definition flow and explains config-as-data vs. drift.
Justifies build/buy per plane (portal, orchestrator, IaC, CD) and chooses a multi-tenancy model with reasons.
Designs ephemeral environments with a reaper and versioned golden paths.
Treats the platform as a product: adoption strategy, paved-roads-with-escape-hatches, and team topology (platform as an enabling team).
Reasons about the orchestrator as a shared failure domain and how to contain it.
Measures success with DORA/SPACE/DevEx and is honest about the DORA 2024 throughput/stability tradeoff.

followUps: How do you migrate 200 existing services onto the platform without a big-bang cutover?
How do you keep the Platform Orchestrator from being a single point of failure for all deploys?
What do you deliberately NOT abstract, and how do you expose an escape hatch for power users?
What's your golden-path deprecation policy when you ship a breaking v2?
How do you prove the platform is working — and what would tell you it's quietly hurting throughput?
How would you decide between buying Humanitec and assembling Score + your own controllers?

links.title: CNCF Platforms White Paper
links.why: Vendor-neutral definition of a platform and its capability domains — the model the five planes formalize.
links.groupDescription: The canonical, vendor-neutral models behind platform engineering.

links.title: Platform Engineering Maturity Model
links.why: Provisional → Operational → Scalable → Optimizing; explains the ticket-ops bottleneck and why only ~13% reach 'Optimizing'.

links.title: How to build an Internal Developer Platform
links.why: The source of the five-plane reference architecture used as this interview's spine (read the numbers as vendor claims).

links.title: Score — workload specification
links.why: The vendor-neutral spec (metadata/containers/service/resources) that is the developer contract.
links.groupDescription: The orchestrator and the developer-facing workload spec at the heart of an IDP.

links.title: What is a Platform Orchestrator?
links.why: Explains Read-Match-Create-Deploy and dynamic configuration — the mechanism that kills config drift.

links.title: Backstage — software catalog & golden paths
links.why: The canonical open-source developer portal: catalog, scaffolder, golden-path templates.
links.groupDescription: Self-service, golden paths, and the team topology that makes a platform work.

links.title: Backstage is not your platform
links.why: Why a portal is a discovery layer, not a provisioning engine — the Step 3 → Step 4 forcing function.

links.title: Thinnest Viable Platform
links.why: Build the minimum platform that accelerates teams; the antidote to over-building and golden cages.

links.title: Full Cycle Developers (paved road)
links.why: The paved road is supported, not mandated — going off-road takes more effort, not less.

links.title: DORA 2024 Accelerate State of DevOps Report
links.why: The honest finding: IDPs raised productivity (+6%) but could lower throughput (−8%) and stability (−14%).

links.title: The SPACE of Developer Productivity
links.why: Measure ≥3 dimensions (Satisfaction, Performance, Activity, Communication, Efficiency) — no single metric works.

links.title: DevEx: What Actually Drives Productivity
links.why: Feedback loops, cognitive load, and flow — the developer-experience lens for a platform.

links.title: Crossplane vs. Terraform/Pulumi
links.why: Control-plane reconciliation vs. apply-time IaC — the cleanest IaC tradeoff to teach.
links.groupDescription: Concrete build/buy tradeoffs and licensing reality for the planes.

links.title: HashiCorp adopts the Business Source License
links.why: The Aug 2023 BSL move that spawned OpenTofu (and Vault → OpenBao); a real build/buy fork in the road.

links.title: Argo CD vs. Flux
links.why: UI + ApplicationSet (Argo) vs. composable RBAC-only controllers (Flux).

links.title: What is GitOps?
links.why: Clean vendor-neutral definition: Git as source of truth + continuous reconciliation.

links.title: Kubernetes multi-tenancy
links.why: The neutral guidance on soft vs. hard isolation — namespaces+RBAC are adequate for trusting teams.

links.title: Tenancy models with vcluster
links.why: Virtual control planes per tenant for stronger isolation (read with a grain of vendor salt).

links.title: External Secrets Operator
links.why: Syncs cloud KMS / Vault secrets into Kubernetes — often makes a self-hosted Vault optional.

links.title: Argo CD ApplicationSet PR generator
links.why: One Application per open PR, auto-deleted on close — the basis for ephemeral preview environments.
